data.title: Altria Among 6 Dividend Kings To Announce Annual Payout Increases In August
data.description:
This is the latest in my series of articles where I provide predictions of annual dividend increases for long-term dividend growth companies. At the end of June, I provided predictions for 14 dividend growth companies that have historically announced annual payout increases in July. In this article I’ll look at another 15 dividend growth companies that I expect will announce their annual dividend increases in August.

In response to one of the comments on last month’s article, I provided a prediction for heavy automotive equipment manufacturer Cummins, Inc. (CMI). My prediction was for an 8.0% - 10.2% increase. On July 15th, the company announced a 9.9% increase to an annualized $8.00 a share for its 20th year of dividend growth. This latest increase gives the company a forward yield of 2.19%.

In addition, West Pharmaceutical Services (WST) announced its 33rd year of dividend growth with a 4.8% boost to an annualized rate of $0.88. The company usually announces the increase in October, but decided to include it as part of 2nd quarter earnings. This boost gives the company a forward yield of 0.36%.

(All yields are based on stock prices approximately 2 hours before the market open on Thursday, July 31st.)

Results for Dividend Increase Announcements from July

Community Financial System, Inc. (CBU) – 34 years of dividend growth

Prediction: 2.2 – 4.3% increase to $1.88 - $1.92

Actual: 2.2% increase to $1.88

Forward yield: 3.53%

This is the 6th straight year of 4-cent annual dividend growth from the New York State-based regional bank.

The Clorox Company (CLX) – 49 years

Prediction: 1.6 – 2.5% increase to $4.96 - $5.00

Actual: 1.6% increase to $4.96

Forward yield: 3.93%

While earnings are rebounding, Clorox is still in the middle of a business transformation and is holding off on returning to normal levels of dividend growth. This is the consumer goods company’s 2nd year of sub-2% dividend increases.

Cintas Corporation (CTAS) – 42 years

Prediction: 14.1 – 16.0% increase to $1.78 - $1.81

Actual: 15.4% increase to $1.80

Forward yield: 0.81%

The business services company continues to grow its dividend in the mid-teen percentages.

Community Trust Bancorp, Inc. (CTBI) – 46 years

Prediction: 4.3 – 6.4% increase to $1.96 - $2.00

Actual: 12.8% increase to $2.12

Forward yield: 3.92%

This is the largest dividend increase from Kentucky-based regional bank since 2004.

Duke Energy Corporation (DUK) – 22 years

Prediction: 1.9 – 2.9% increase to $4.26 - $4.30

Actual: 1.9% increase to $4.26

Forward yield: 3.55%

The utility’s payout ratio above 70% continues to limit dividend growth to 2 – 3%.

Greene County Bancorp, Inc. (GCBC) – 13 years

Prediction: 11.1 – 16.7% increase to $0.40 - $0.42

Actual: 11.1% increase to $0.40

Forward yield: 1.66%

Earnings growth at the upstate New York bank continues to power double-digit dividend growth.

McKesson Corporation (MCK) – 19 years

Prediction: 11.1 – 14.4% increase to $3.15 - $3.25

Actual: 15.5% increase to $3.28

Forward yield: 0.46%

McKesson continues to grow its dividend by double-digit percentages.

Note: In the previous article, I had the wrong dividend payment for McKesson. I’ve adjusted the dividend amounts above.

Mondelez International, Inc. (MDLZ) – 13 years

Prediction: 9.6 – 11.7% increase to $2.06 - $2.12

Actual: 6.4% increase to $2.00

Forward yield: 3.09%

Dividend growth at the snack and candy company fell from 10% last year to this year’s 6.4% increase due to higher cocoa prices hitting earnings.

Marsh & McLennan Companies, Inc. (MMC) – 17 years

Prediction: 11.7 – 14.1% increase to $3.64 - $3.72

Actual: 11.1% increase to $3.60

Forward yield: 1.80%

The insurance company continues to compound its dividend at 11%.

PPG Industries, Inc. (PPG) – 55 years

Prediction: 3.7 – 5.1% increase to $2.82 - $2.86

Actual: 4.4% increase to $2.84

Forward yield: 2.70%

Dividend growth continues to slow at the paint and coatings company in response to slowing earnings.

Republic Services, Inc. (RSG) – 23 years

Prediction: 6.0 – 7.8% increase to $2.46 - $2.50

Actual: 7.8% increase to $2.50

Forward yield: 1.08%

Waste company Republic Services hit the high end of my expectations with an 8% boost.

The J. M. Smucker Company (SJM) – 29 years

Prediction: 1.9 – 2.8% increase to $4.40 - $4.44

Actual: 1.9% increase to $4.40

Forward yield: 4.03%

The food company continues to focus on paying down debt rather than on fast dividend growth.

Stanley Black & Decker, Inc. (SWK) – 59 years

Prediction: 0.6 – 1.8% increase to $3.30 - $3.34

Actual: 1.2% increase to $x3.32

Forward yield: 4.93%

This is the 2nd year of 1% dividend growth as the tool company continues to restructure its operations.

Skyworks Solutions, Inc. (SWKS) – 11 years

Prediction: 1.4 – 2.9% increase to $2.84 - $2.88

Actual: Deferred to August

The semiconductor developer should announce its next dividend increase when it reports earnings on August 5th.

Predictions for Dividend Increases in August

There are 15 long-term dividend growth companies I expect to announce their annual increases in August. First, here is my prediction for one featured company:

Altria Group, Inc. (MO) – 55 years

With a dividend yield around 7% and a growth history of more than half a century, making it a Dividend King, Altria is an income investor’s dream. The tobacco company has three business segments, including two reportable segments: Smokeable Products and Oral Tobacco Products. The Smokeable Products segment covers Altria’s cigarette and cigar business, under multiple brands including Marlboro cigarettes and Black & Mild cigars. The Oral Tobacco Products segment includes sales of Copenhagen, Skoal, on! and Red Seal tobacco dip and pouches. The third segment is “All Other”, which includes joint ventures and investments such as the NJOY brand of e-vapor cigarettes, the company’s investments in Anheuser-Busch InBev and Cronos, and the company’s research and development.

As smoking and tobacco use have fallen in the United States, sales have stayed flat or fallen slightly. The sales drop for the Smokeable Products and Oral Tobacco segments ranges from a 1% drop in sales volume for the combined oral tobacco products to a 10% drop in cigarettes. Recognizing this, Altria has shifted its business mission to transitioning smokers to what the company calls less harmful choices, encapsulated in the motto “Moving Beyond Smoking”. Over the last several years, the company has invested in alternatives to traditional tobacco products. For example, in 2022 Altria entered into a joint venture with a subsidiary of Japan Tobacco to market heated tobacco stick products in the U. S. and in 2023 Altria finalized its acquisition of e-vapor company NJOY Holdings.

The company’s done an impressive job continuing to grow earnings in what is an admittedly hostile environment for tobacco use. EPS growth in 2024 was 3.4%, which the company followed up with a 6% growth in the first half of 2025. The company also continues to buy back shares – after repurchasing more than 9% of its outstanding shares since 2020, Altria recently authorized an additional $1 billion share buyback program. There are some headwinds that the company is facing – after divesting itself of its investment in JUUL, the U. S. International Trade Commission issued an import ban on NJOY vaping devices and pods in March due to a patent infringement ruling in favor of Juul Labs. However, the company’s Oral Tobacco segment represents about 10% of total company sales and less than 15% of company income so, although sales of NJOY products are on a downward trajectory, this should have little impact on Altria as a whole. With EPS growth, Altria will be able to extend its dividend growth record to 56 years with a mid-single digit boost.

Prediction: 3.9 – 5.9% increase to $4.24 - $4.32

Predicted Forward Yield: 6.98 – 7.11%

Here are my predictions for 14 other long-term dividend growth companies which should announce annual increases in August:

Company # Yrs Industry Prediction (%) New Annual Rate
American States Water (AWR) 71 Utilities - Regulated Water 8.5% - 10.6% $2.02 - $2.06
The California-based utility has the distinction of having the longest dividend growth streak among publicly traded companies. The company provides both water and electrical utility services, with the size of its water business being far larger than the electricity business. Business is good, with 2024 EPS growth of 11% and first quarter 2025 EPS growth of 13% far surpassing the company’s long term dividend growth of 8 – 9%. With the good EPS growth, I expect this year’s dividend growth to be on the high side of that range, with the chance of something slightly larger. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.75 – 2.80%
BancFirst Corporation (BANF) 32 Financial - Regional Banks 6.5% - 7.6% $1.96 - $1.98
BancFirst Corporation is the bank holding company for three separate bank chains: BancFirst Bank in Oklahoma, and Pegasus Bank and Worthington Bank in Texas; as well as independent insurance agency BancFirst Insurance Services. The company’s sales have shot up over the last several years, but over the last year operating expenses grew just as fast, resulting in 2024 EPS growth of less than 2%. 2025 is shaping up to be a better year, with 11% EPS growth in the first quarter of the year. With a payout ratio below 30%, the company can maintain its 7% dividend growth rate despite the lack of earnings growth. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.57 – 1.58%
Badger Meter, Inc. (BMI) 33 Technology - Scientific and Technical Instruments 25.0% - 29.4% $1.70 - $1.76
Designer and manufacturer of water management products Badger Meter had a very good 2024, and it looks like 2025 is going to be good also. The company grew sales 18% and EPS 35% in 2024, and is following up with another 31% EPS growth in the first quarter of 2025. This small-cap company is debt free and hasn’t been repurchasing its shares, meaning that the additional income is entirely available for dividend growth. Badger’s situation was similar last year and the company came through with a 26% dividend boost. I’m expecting another increase at the same rate or even close to 30% this year. Predicted Forward Yield: 0.89 – 0.92%
Broadridge Financial Solutions (BR) 18 Technology - Information Technology Services 10.2% - 11.4% $3.88 - $3.92
The financial technology leader routinely grows its dividend at 10%+ year-over-year, powered by double-digit earnings growth. Adjusted EPS grew by 10.3% in 2024 and is expected to grow another 8 – 12% in 2025, with the company posting 18% growth in the first three quarters of the fiscal year. The company doesn’t spend its free cash flow on share buybacks so the company can focus on growing the business and boosting its payout, meaning that investors can look forward to another double-digit boost. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.57 – 1.59%
CBOE Global Markets, Inc. (CBOE) 15 Financial - Financial Data & Stock Markets 12.7% - 15.9% $2.84 - $2.92
CBOE runs one of the world’s largest derivatives and securities exchange network. Originating from the Chicago Board Options Exchange, the company has seen dramatic volume growth in stock options, which has been a major part of its earnings growth. Adjusted EPS growth came in at 10% in 2024, with an additional 16% growth in the first quarter of 2025. Investors can expect dividend growth to be around the same level as last year’s 14.5% boost. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.18 – 1.21%
Chemed Corporation (CHE) 17 Healthcare - Medical Care Facilities 7.0% - 9.0% $2.14 - $2.18
Chemed has two primary business lines: it operates the VITAS Healthcare Corporation, which owns facilities that provide end-of-life care and it owns and operates the Roto-Rooter drain cleaning provider. About 2/3rds of the company’s revenues and earnings come from VITAS. VITAS acquired the health care facilities of Covenant Health in April 2024, helping to boost earnings by 10% in 2024. However, the company recently reduced its 2025 guidance due to revenue limitations from the cap on Medicare reimbursements. With the uncertainty, investors can expect a slowdown from last year’s 15% dividend boost to something closer to the company’s 8% long-term growth rate. Predicted Forward Yield: 0.51 – 0.52%
Carlisle Companies Incorporated (CSL) 49 Industrials - Building Products & Equipment 17.0% - 19.0% $4.68 - $4.76
Once an industrial conglomerate, Carlisle has transformed itself into a building products company, focusing on weatherproofing and construction materials. The company has divested all its non-building products businesses and acquired some new companies that fit into the plan. It’s used excess cash flow to buy back more than 18% of its shares since 2020 and built a long-term dividend growth rate in the mid-teen percentages. It looks like investors can expect the dividend growth to continue – despite flat revenues and a small drop in adjusted EPS in the first half of 2025, business in 2024 was outstanding with revenue growth of 9% and adjusted EPS growth of 30%. With a payout ratio around 20%, Carlisle will be able to match last year’s high teen percentage payout boost this year. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.25 – 1.27%
Dover Corporation (DOV) 70 Industrials - Specialty Industrial Machinery 1.0% - 1.9% $2.08 - $2.10
This industrial conglomerate is known for its lengthy dividend growth record, second only to American States Water Company among publicly traded companies. Dover’s dividend growth has slowed over the last decade, with the company boosting its annual dividend by only 2 cents over each of the last 5 years. That may be changing. Dover’s gone on a purchasing spree, acquiring 8 companies over the last 4 years. This is starting to show up in earnings, with 2024 adjusted EPS growth of 4% and expectations of 12% in 2025. But until the expectations are realized, dividend growth rates should stay flat. I expect another 2-cent increase, with the possibility of a 4-cent increase. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.14 – 1.15%
Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT) 58 Real Estate - Retail REIT 0.5% - 1.4% $4.42 - $4.46
The retail REIT has been going through some tough times. Over each of the last 5 years, the company has grown its dividend by only 4 cents, and growth in funds from operations (FFO, a measure of REIT profitability that accounts for the tax benefits of real estate ownership) has been in the 3 – 4% range recently, not enough for faster dividend growth. FFO growth was 3.4% in 2024 and 3.7% in the first quarter of 2025. With a growing share count (up 12% since 2020), investors can expect another year of slow dividend growth. Predicted Forward Yield: 4.72 – 4.77%
Ingredion Incorporated (INGR) 15 Consumer Defensive - Packaged Foods 3.8% - 5.0% $3.32 - $3.36
Ingredion manufactures and markets food additives like coloring and sweeteners to food and beverage companies. While the new presidential administration has made one of its lines of efforts the reduction in artificial additives, business at Ingredion is booming, with the company posting 13% adjusted EPS growth in 2024 and expectations of additional growth of up to 9% in 2025. While the company has an active share buyback program – having repurchased 4% of its shares since 2020 – I think the company will accelerate its dividend growth from last year’s 2.6% boost. Look for an increase in the mid-single digit range. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.51 – 2.54%
Intuit Inc. (INTU) 14 Technology - Software 20.2% - 23.1% $5.00 - $5.12
Known by most people for their Turbo Tax personal tax filing software, Intuit has multiple software products that are all going gangbusters – Credit Karma, QuickBooks and Mailchimp are all seeing double-digit growth in both sales and profits. Intuit grew its 2024 EPS by 24% to $10.43 and raised its 2025 EPS growth guidance throughout the year to 26%. The company’s established a good long term growth rate of close to 20% over the last decade and with last year’s and this year’s EPS growth, investors can expect an increase around that amount, if not higher. Predicted Forward Yield: 0.62 – 0.63%
MGE Energy, Inc. (MGEE) 50 Utilities - Regulated Electric 2.2% - 4.4% $1.84 - $1.88
Earnings posted by the Wisconsin-based gas and electric utility move essentially with energy usage and is dramatically affected by the weather in the mid-western United States. 2024 EPS grew by only 2.5% as warmer-than-normal weather reduced gas demand. However, as weather in the first quarter of 2025 cooled down relative to the same period in 2024, EPS jumped more than 20%. MGE Energy has a fairly consistent dividend growth rate of 5%, but last year’s EPS growth means that this year’s dividend boost will be smaller. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.20 – 2.25%
Nordson Corporation (NDSN) 62 Industrials - Specialty Industrial Machinery 10.9% - 12.8% $3.46 - $3.52
The industrial conglomerate has consistently put up dividend growth in the teens, sporting a compounded growth rate of 14% over the last decade. But while Nordson has been able to grow sales in the mid-single digit percentages, increased interest expense is impacting the bottom line, with adjusted EPS down 1% in 2024 and 1.5% in the first half of 2025. Nordson continues to generate good free cash flow, but with the slowdown in earnings, I expect that this year’s dividend increase will be a little below the average. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.60 – 1.63%
Westlake Chemical Corporation (WLK) 21 Basic Materials - Specialty Chemicals 6.7% - 8.6% $2.24 - $2.28
Westlake has two primary business units – Performance and Essential Materials, which operates its polyethylene and vinyl chemical production and Housing and Infrastructure Products, which manufactures products for building materials. While the company posted EPS of $4.64, up 25% from the previous year, it’s a far cry from the EPS of $17.35 the company posted in 2022. While the company is turning the corner, it won’t be able to build on its 15% compounded growth rate over the last 5 years. Instead, I expect a slight increase from last year’s 5% boost into the high single digits. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.81 – 2.86%

Summary

It was a mixed bag for dividend growth investors in July. We saw five companies reward investors with 10%+ increases, led by medical distributor McKesson and business services company Cintas with a 15% boost. As always, there were companies that are either going through business transformations or just simply suffering from a business slowdown that grow their dividends by small amounts to keep their dividend growth streak going. Included in this category are consumer products company Clorox, tool company Stanley Black & Decker, food manufacturer J. M. Smucker, and utility Duke Energy, each of which announced sub-2% increases.

Coming up in August, we have several Dividend Kings that will announce their annual increases, including two companies with records of dividend growth for seven decades. American States Water and Dover Corporation, having grown their payouts for 71 and 70 years respectively, have the two longest dividend growth streaks among publicly traded companies. There are four other companies with 50+ year records of payout growth, putting them in the “Dividend King” category: Federal Realty Corporation, Altria, Nordson Corporation, and MGE Energy.

I have high expectations for dividend growth in August. We should see double-digit percentage growth from 6 or 7 companies, including CBOE, Carlisle Companies, Intuit, Broadridge, Nordson, Badger Meter, and possibly American States Water. In contrast, we’ll see 1 – 2% increases from Dover Coporation (yet another year of small growth from them) and Federal Realty. We’ll also see 7 – 8% increases from chemical company Westlake, medical care facility operator Chemed, and bank holding company BancFirst.

data.title: Core PCE Inflation Rises 2.8% In June, Higher Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Fed’s preferred inflation gauge, the core PCE price index, rose 2.8% year-over-year in June. This was higher than the expected 2.7% growth and a slight pickup from May. On a monthly basis, the core index was up 0.3%, as expected.

The headline index was up 2.6% year-over-year. This was just above the expected 2.5% annual growth and up from 2.4% in May. On a monthly basis, the index was up 0.3%, as expected..

PCE Price Index

Personal consumption expenditures (PCE) measures and tracks changes for all domestic personal consumption. Core PCE measures the changes in personal consumption less food and energy, making it less volatile than the headline PCE. The PCE Price Index is calculated using PCE data and is a key way to measure changes in purchasing trends and inflation.

The adjacent thumbnail gives us a close-up of the trend in YoY core PCE since January 2012. The first string of red data points highlights the 12 consecutive months when core PCE hovered in a narrow range around its interim low. The second string highlights the lower range from late 2014 through 2015. Core PCE shifted higher in 2016 with a decline in 2017, 2019, and 2020, with a major jump in 2022.

The next chart below shows the monthly year-over-year change in the personal consumption expenditures (PCE) price index since 2000. Also included is an overlay of the core PCE (less food and energy) price index, which is the Fed's preferred indicator for gauging inflation. The 2% benchmark is the Fed's conventional target for core inflation.

For a long-term perspective, here are the same two metrics spanning five decades.

Inflation: Fed's 2% Target

The Fed traditionally uses the PCE Price Index as their preferred inflation gauge; however, another common measure of inflation is the Consumer Price Index.

In their latest meeting, the Fed held rates steady at 4.25-4.50%, as expected. This marked the fifth straight meeting the Fed has left rates unchanged. The statement from the meeting revealed the Committee believes inflation remains "somewhat elevated" but that they are strongly committed to returning inflation to its 2% objective.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Franklin Small Cap Growth Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: US stocks advanced during the second quarter of 2025. After rebounding from April's lows, equity markets continued to rally through June despite bouts of volatility. The S&P 500 ((SP500), (SPX)) Index and Nasdaq (NDAQ) Composite Index closed the period with solid gains after hitting new record highs at the end of June, while the Dow (DOW) Jones Industrial Average hovered near its all-time high. Temporary delays in tariff hikes, reduced fears of a recession, growing expectations of interest-rate cuts later in the year and easing geopolitical tensions helped drive US stocks higher. Large-capitalization equities gained the most, followed by mid- and small-cap stocks, and growth stocks outperformed value by a wide margin in all three market-cap tiers.

  • Detractors: Stock selection effects were the primary cause of the fund's underperformance of the Russell 2000 Growth Index benchmark. Stock selection in the information technology (IT) sector was a leading relative detractor. Stock selection and an overweight in the consumer staples sector and stock selection in the energy sector also hindered relative performance.

  • Contributors: The bulk of the fund's relative strength came from stock selection and an underweight in the health care sector. To a lesser degree, the fund benefited from stock selection in the financials and consumer discretionary sectors. Overall, sector allocation decisions worked in our favor.

  • Outlook: We are hopeful as we enter the second half of the year that financial markets can focus on the potential long-term structural benefits of a Trump 2.0 policy agenda that might include initiatives to rebalance global trade and stimulate growth through tax reform and deregulation.

Performance Review

  • The fund's return was positive but lagged that of the Russell 2000 Growth Index. Relative returns in the IT sector were negatively impacted by stock selection in the semiconductors and semiconductor equipment industry, where Onto Innovation (ONTO) was a leading detractor. The process control solutions company issued disappointing second-quarter earnings guidance that weighed on its shares. We believe Onto operates in an attractive space (process control, metrology, inspection, lithography) during a time when semiconductor packaging complexity is increasing dramatically.

  • Nutrition products company BellRing Brands (BRBR) was a top detractor in the consumer staples sector. While the company reported strong performance across its protein-focused portfolio, it raised concerns about inventory reductions and profit-margin pressures in the second half of the year. We believe BellRing continues to benefit from various tailwinds, including new capacity, improved inventory tracking and ramped up promotions and marketing.

  • In contrast, in the industrials sector, BWX Technologies (BWXT) was a leading individual contributor to relative returns. The company is a supplier of nuclear components and fuel to the US Navy and the commercial nuclear power industry. We believe BWX can profit from the government's focus on bolstering defense spending.

Outlook

  • Our outlook for financial markets remains cautious, reflecting the magnitude and complexity of the current political and economic climate. While challenging, active management allows us to pursue alpha by taking advantage of market volatility as a chance to initiate or build up positions in what we believe are high-quality businesses. These businesses have strong balance sheets and market-leading competitive positions, and they are levered to durable secular growth themes.

  • One theme is technology, which continues to be a large area of opportunity as more companies adopt artificial intelligence in their efforts to lower costs and increase productivity. We see significant growth potential for the industrials sector, fueled by trends that include the reshoring of US manufacturing, electrification and meaningful infrastructure investment. Our outlook for health care is also bullish. Wide-ranging innovations (genomics, robotics, personalized medicine) and meaningful demographic shifts support our convictions.

  • Deregulation could positively impact the energy sector by increasing fossil fuel production and streamlining the permitting process. Deregulation and lower taxes could serve many companies in the financials sector as they could help boost profits. With a stronger economy and lower regulatory burden, we think banks may see an increase in lending activity, and fintech innovation could surge.

  • Our strategy is designed to address both upward and downward market cycles, and a diversified approach helps us participate in both ongoing market strength and anticipated rotation in market leadership. We invest in what we regard as great businesses positioned to potentially benefit from secular growth over a long-term horizon.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Small- and mid-cap stocks involve greater risks and volatility than large-cap stocks. The investment style may become out of favor, which may have a negative impact on performance. To the extent the portfolio invests in a concentration of certain securities, regions or industries, it is subject to increased volatility. Liquidity risk exists when securities or other investments become more difficult to sell, or are unable to be sold, at the price at which they have been valued. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance (ESG) criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identified or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund's prospectus.

Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) is an unmanaged index composed of 30 blue-chip stocks, each with annual sales exceeding $7 billion. data.title: Franklin U.S. Government Ladder 1-5 Year SMA Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: There were large swings in financial market sentiment across the second quarter of 2025. During April, fixed income spreads generally widened amid US President Donald Trump's "Liberation Day" tariff announcements due to fears that they could lead to a drop in growth and a new round of inflationary pressures. A subsequent 90-day pause for many of the increased levies helped calm market nerves. The passage of the "One Big Beautiful Bill" spending package after quarter-end further helped reduce fixed income market uncertainties. Over the period, US Treasury (UST) yield changes were mixed. Very short- and intermediate-dated yields witnessed declines, while longer-maturity UST yields increased. Yields declined by 16 basis points (BPS) to 3.72% on the two-year UST note (US2Y) and by 15 bps to 3.80% on the five-year UST note (US5Y).
  • Contributors: There were no material contributors to returns.
  • Detractors: Exposure to the two to five year portion of the UST yield curve.
  • Outlook: The US Federal Reserve's (Fed's) reaction function is complicated. Fears of increased inflation due to fiscal policy are likely to keep the Fed on hold, in our assessment. We are looking for at most one rate cut for the remainder of the year.

Performance Review

  • Yield curve positioning hurt the strategy's results due to an overweight to duration exposure in the two to five year portion of the curve as UST yields rose.

Outlook

  • In our view, the Fed is unlikely to cut interest rates soon. Despite recent signs of cooling inflation, Fed officials remain cautious due to ongoing tariff-related uncertainties. Companies are expected to raise prices as pre-tariff inventories diminish, leading to a likely rise in core goods inflation. Furthermore, average US tariff rates remain historically high despite a temporary easing in US-China trade tensions. We therefore expect potentially higher inflation and slower growth. With inflation still above target and risks skewed to the upside, we expect the Fed to maintain its pause. Amid still-resilient retail sales, we believe the Fed is likely to stay on hold until there is clear evidence of labor market deterioration without a simultaneous rise in inflation.
  • The "One Big Beautiful Bill" spending package will further increase the US deficit over the next decade. Combined with factors such as uncertain trade policy (which has raised stagflationary concerns) and an expansionary fiscal policy (which implies greater debt issuance), we expect short-term rates to remain elevated due to the pause in Fed rate cuts and longer-term rates to continue to climb higher through the rest of the year.

data.title: Reinsurance Group of America: Growth Focus May Be Backfiring (Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Reinsurance Group of America, Incorporated (NYSE:RGA) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing 15% of their value. While it has been aggressive in signing large transactions, mixed performance has increased concerns about the efficacy of its capital allocation efforts. Another mixed quarter in Q2 is only likely to sustain those fears with the stock down over 5% after hours.

I last covered shares of RGA in March, reiterating them as a Buy. Since then, they are up just 2%, missing out on a 12% market rally. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit RGA.

In the company’s second quarter, Reinsurance Group of America earned just $4.72, which missed expectations by $0.83. This was down substantially from the $5.66 it earned in Q1 because RGA experienced unfavorably high claims in U.S. life and healthcare. While claims can be volatile quarter to quarter, especially in large-value life insurance, this continues a string of volatile results, which is all the more concerning given the pace of recent growth. Sometimes when an insurer is growing very fast, it can actually be a warning sign that it is selling policies too cheaply, and that concern is likely to build after these results.

In total, RGA had $256 million of unfavorable claims in Q2, equivalent to about 3% of premiums, a significant variance that more than fully offset ~$160 million of Q1 favorability. This most notably impacted U.S. and Latin American traditional, which barely turned a profit, earning just $4 million, down from $167 million last year. This unit accounts for about a quarter of premiums. A favorable investment environment continues to support U.S. financial solutions (which grew earnings by 20%), and solutions across most geographies. Asia was the only region where it had favorable claims experience in its traditional business.

This large underwriting miss comes as RGA’s traditional premiums are growing 11% from last year on a constant currency basis. They accounted for about 90% of premiums in Q2 because pension-risk transfer premiums inside its financial solutions units are very lumpy. With growth accelerating so much, it is critical to see these recent deals perform well, and another quarter of unfavorable claims will increase worries that growth is so high because the company is underpricing risk.

In fact, even as premiums are rising, the company’s returns have been coming down, adding to the evidence that recent policies are not performing to expectations. RGA generated just a 12.7% return on equity (“ROE”) in Q2, below its 13-15% target. Now, its trailing 12-month ROE is within range but has been ticking down for over a year. At 14.3%, RGA’s ROE is the lowest in two years and likely to decline further.

This compression in ROE is especially concerning because it coincides with RGA relying on more financial leverage, which all else equal should enhance ROE. While RGA has been holding debt to capital constant, it has been increasingly relying on hybrids (debt-like securities that get some equity treatment in rating agency calculations) to finance growth. Frankly, I would like to see RGA slow the pace of new business, digest its recent and pending deals, and bring total debt to capital back below 33%.

Now in Q2, the company deployed just $276 million of capital into new deals, but that is because one of the most significant deals in the company’s history was pending. In addition to announcing earnings, on July 31st, RGA announced it closed its $32 billion reinsurance transaction with Equitable (EQH). Given the deal closed subsequent to quarter end, it did not impact Q2 results. This is a megadeal, with RGA reinsuring 75% of EQH’s individual life block. As part of this deal, RGA will manage 30% of the assets, with 70% still to be managed by EQH’s controlled subsidiary, Alliance Bernstein (AB). RGA is deploying $1.5 billion in capital to support this transaction.

This deal is expected to generate $70 million of operating income in 2025, $160-$170 million in 2026, and $200 million over time once the asset portfolio is fully reinvested according to RGA’s strategy. This implies a 13.5-14% return on capital once ramped, but the deal will be dilutive to ROE until 2027. It is absolutely essential for management credibility to achieve these targets. With many assets still managed by AB, I am cautious on achieving its run-rate and see downside risk to RGA’s modelling, especially given recent performance.

Now back in February, RGA expected to have just $800 million of deployable capital in 2025 after the EQH deal—meaning this one deal accounts for about 2/3 of its new business this year. However given debt movements and how it values its in-force book, RGA now estimates it has $2.3 billion of deployable capital after the EQH deal.

Based on this significant upward revision, it seems like RGA’s appetite for large deals is not diminishing. While this increase is intended to be shown as a positive, speaking to RGA’s capacity for growth, I am not certain it will be perceived that way, as further premium growth may come at the expense of returns, based on recent trends.

Now, as with any insurer, RGA maintains a large investment portfolio, and while it manages the portfolio a bit more aggressively to generate returns, the portfolio is still fairly conservative. RGA has also been benefiting from the rate environment. Its new money investment yield was 6.53% whereas it had a 4.98% portfolio investment yield. Given ongoing reinvestment and new money, this yield was up 22bps from a year ago.

RGA has a $115 billion investment portfolio. $86 billion of this portfolio is in fixed maturity bonds, 94% of which are investment grade. As I said, it is a high-quality portfolio. RGA has a reasonable 3% allocation to alternatives.

Given challenges in commercial real estate (“CRE”), I would note that RGA has $10 billion of commercial mortgage exposure. The portfolio has a 60% loan-to-value and 1.75x debt-service coverage. These are strong ratios. About $1.75 billion of this portfolio is in office where it has a 64% average LTV. Plus, just 11% of the portfolio matures over the next 18 months, reducing rollover risk. I do not see substantial credit risk here.

Alongside results, RGA raised its dividend by 4.5%, giving shares a 2% dividend yield, which is supported by $550 million of liquidity at the holding company. Given its focus on organic growth, capital returns beyond the dividend are limited. Its share count is down only about 1.4% from last year.

Back in March, I assigned a price target of $213, given my view that RGA could generate a 13-14% ROE over the medium term, at the low end of its 13-15% target. With a book value of $156x, this still implies a fair value of at least $203. However, I see an increasing risk that RGA fails to meet this target, given the seemingly dilutive nature of recent deals. We also know that the EQH deal will struggle to achieve return targets for at least a year.

With such fast growth, rising all-in leverage, and questionable deal economics, the RGA investment thesis feels increasingly broken, and it is difficult for me to see a near-term change in this dynamic. Management has an increasing credibility problem, and it will take several quarters to resolve this. As such, I see shares likely to be stuck in the $175-$190 range, essentially haircutting fair value by 10-15% to reflect this added overhang.

As such, I am moving shares to a Hold. After this quarter, I struggled to continue to defend RGA’s performance; indeed, I defended it too long. With so many other options in the market, I see no compelling reason to buy shares at this juncture, and I would stay on the sidelines in RGA.

data.title: Amazon Q2: Almost Flawless Performance, Misunderstood By The Market
data.description:
A huge e-commerce event, more than 1 million robots, various AI-related features, high viewership for the NASCAR Cup on its streaming service, and advances in its satellite Internet project. That's not all, but these are factors present in Amazon.com, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:AMZN) thesis and its Q2 results.

It can be overwhelming to try to envision Amazon's future, as there are so many divisions and points that can generate up-selling, cross-selling, and efficiency amid secular trends that it is difficult to have a consolidated view of Amazon, and we often miss some points. Much of this ends up becoming optionality. If in 10 years Amazon is one of the main beneficiaries of trends that are currently very intangible, such as humanoid robots, great, because we don't need to incorporate that into the calculation for its valuation to make sense.

Given all these positive characteristics, which showed good progress in Q2, along with the beat in revenue and EPS, I imagined that Amazon shares would be rising in after-hours trading, as was the case with Meta (META) and Microsoft (MSFT), which also surprised positively and posted substantial gains.

However, the market punished AMZN stock for its below-expectations guidance for Q3, falling about -4% in after-hours trading (at least now as I write this analysis). And even though it's not so encouraging in some ways, it ends up creating a window to accumulate more stocks and reiterate the Buy rating for a company that continues to perform very well, has a lot to gain from secular trends, perhaps even more than other companies, and still has a very interesting margin of safety.

It Seems That The Market Is Overreacting to Amazon's Earnings

In Q2, Amazon reported revenue of $167.7 billion, while estimates were $162 billion. A good beat of 3%. Adjusted EPS was $1.68 against $1.33 expected, a very interesting beat of more than 20%. Furthermore, as the table below shows, the midpoint of guidance for Q3 revenue was $3.5 billion above market expectations.

The problem for the market was operating income. As the midpoint of guidance was $18 billion, and the market expected $19.4 billion, the reaction was negative. But note that the guidance range goes up to $20.5 billion, meaning that even without beating the guidance itself, it would be possible to exceed these expectations. For comparison, Q2 operating income was $19.2 billion, a strong growth of over 30%, while the guidance itself considered a top of $17.5 billion.

Of course, I'm not saying that the company will always exceed the market or its own outlook, nor that this miss in operating income guidance is low, but note that this makes it seem like an overreaction on the part of the market, since there are so many positive points in the quarter and yet the stock is being punished. Not to mention the good reaction of companies with a similar profile, such as Microsoft.

To be honest, even if it were a slight miss in revenue and EPS, it would not make much difference to the long-term thesis, as the operational and positive signs from various initiatives show that Amazon is on track to continue unlocking a lot of value for shareholders.

Thinking Long Term, Q2 Earnings Were Great

The table below suggests to me that earnings were very good. Revenue in North America grew 11%, above recent quarters, as did the International segments. AWS maintained momentum with 17%. Everything is in good shape.

However, that is not the main reason I liked the quarter. For me, one of the main areas where the company will unlock value is in margins. Note how North America's operating margin has already reached 7.5%, a margin close to Q4 2024 but without the boost from seasonality. The same positive movement occurred for the International segment, which has already surpassed last Q4's margin. This illustrates that revenue is moving toward a more profitable mix, such as advertising, services, and subscriptions, which grew more than segments such as online stores, and shows that efficiency gains are ongoing.

The most modest aspect of the result was AWS's operating margin, which fell to around 33%, a significant decline from what the company had been reporting in recent quarters. But this is not so alarming because it was already on the radar. In the Q1 earnings call, the CFO answered a question about AWS's margin in a way that suggested some pressure throughout 2025:

“We did a strong quarter in AWS, as you mentioned, the margin performance. I would attribute it to the strong growth that we're seeing coupled with the impact of some continued investment we're making in innovation and technology, give you some examples. So, we invest in software and process improvements and it ends up optimizing our server capacity, which helps our infrastructure cost. We've been developing more efficient network using our low cost custom networking gear. We're working to maximize the power usage in our existing data centers, which both lowers our costs and also reclaims power for other newer workloads.

And we're also seeing the impact of advancing custom silicon like Graviton. It provides lower costs not only for us, but also for our customers, the better price performance for them. But you're right, margins are impacted by a lot of things, including our level of investment, competitive pricing, the mix of Generative AI services as they're ramping up will continue to evolve over the years to come. So, we do have a lot of investment in infrastructure going on and planned for the second-half of the year. So, that will -- we'll start to see the impact of that. But we're happy with the performance of the team with generating cost savings. And it's a big focus as well as expanding the services and features for customers.”

And if we compare, AWS's growth was not as impressive as Azure's, but that does not invalidate the fact that AWS is also (perhaps even better than its peers) very well positioned to continue riding the secular trend of AI and cloud growth.

And that's not the only reason to have Amazon; in fact, as mentioned at the beginning of the analysis, there are countless reasons. In its earnings release, Amazon cited some of the key events of the quarter:

  • Biggest Prime Day event ever;
  • Expansion of same-day and next-day delivery;
  • New partnerships;
  • Launched GenAI tools, expanded Alexa+ Early Access;
  • Increased Project Kuiper's satellite internet fleet with two successful launches;
  • Had 2 million viewers per race in Prime Video’s NASCAR Cup;
  • Innovated with Amazon Nova (AI platform to build AI agents and similars); and
  • Introduced Vulcan, Amazon’s robot with sight and touch.

And that’s not all, there is an even longer list to check out and delve into to understand each Amazon tool, initiative, and/or robot.

Vulcan Is An Example Of The Future And The Strength Of Amazon

All of this shows me that Amazon's revenue and margin expansion is far from over. Vulcan is a very good example of this thesis. Unlike humanoid robots such as Tesla's (TSLA) Optimus, it does not attract as much attention for its appearance, nor does it seem as disruptive as it really is. But it turns out that in addition to being very disruptive, it also has the characteristic of bringing more efficiency to the company.

Its main difference is that with the use of AI, Vulcan has the “power of touch” to better handle products and even learn from its own mistakes. This touch that Vulcan has was something that even Amazon engineers were skeptical about achieving, something that seemed very difficult, but now it is there and working.

The trend of AI continuing to evolve greatly helps Amazon's revenue and margin prospects, and as I mentioned, it is even difficult to trace all the implications. Efficiency in logistics and improving AWS services and those provided to third-party sellers are more obvious, but we can still consider optionalities such as the evolution of content production for Prime Video, or adding even more features that allow for the charging of subscription services.

In short, Amazon is one of the leading companies in capturing all these innovative trends. The future is truly promising for the company, and this quarter shows that the company knows this and knows how to execute very well.

In fact, in addition to classic risks such as uncertainties regarding tariffs and competition from players such as Mercado Libre (MELI) for the e-commerce division and with Microsoft in the cloud area, one of the main concerns for the thesis, in my view, is that Amazon will become “too big,” as this could trigger even more regulatory problems and other dilemmas.

Amazon Also Offers A Good Margin Of Safety

Even amid this movement that prices companies positioned in these technology trends very well, Amazon manages to offer an interesting margin of safety. While companies like Microsoft are trading above their historical price-to-earnings average, Amazon is trading below it.

Note in the table below that even though it still has a lot to unlock in its fundamentals, multiples such as price-to-earnings, EV-to-EBITDA, and even price-to-cash-flow are substantially lower than their average over the last five years.

Being simplistic in valuation, think that a multiple of 35x forward earnings or even a little less is fair for Amazon. If this multiple remains constant and net income continues to grow above 10% in the coming years, this is more or less the expected total return, since if well priced, the stock price in the long term follows earnings.

One of the relevant points worth mentioning is that it does not seem feasible to imagine that the company will return to trading at astronomical multiples, since now its fundamentals are much more mature, with the operating margin of all segments having already advanced well, i.e., we will not see the margin of the international division double again, but rather an advance more in line with its current maturity.

The Bottom Line

Amazon is one of the leading companies for exposure to long-term trends in a very efficient and diversified manner. As Q2 earnings showed, the company is not only already delivering significant progress in fundamentals, but also has many projects that will continue to yield results in the coming years and decades.

Therefore, I reiterate my buy rating for Amazon.

data.title: This Is Why It's Time To Trim Your AMD Stock Ahead Of Q2 Earnings (Downgrade)
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) stock, it is up by 30% in price.

AMD has earnings coming up on August 5th, and the reality is that even with a modest beat and raise, the stock is going to experience a minor pullback. That's why I trimmed my AMD position by 1/3 on Monday July 28, and sold the rest on Wednesday July 30. My total return since buying in H1 2025 was about 75% for the final closed stake.

It's best not to be greedy—those are world-class returns and sentiment-value investors like myself know that the massive AMD value opportunity is over and was prime in March following an extended downtrend. AMD will almost certainly continue to rise over the next few years, but with volatility. I think H2 2025 will be a period of potentially a tech and general equity market pullback/consolidation period before a stronger 2026. I'm rotating proceeds from trimmed AI names into more defensive and cyclical value plays right now as a result.

Here's What You Need To Know

AMD stock is currently trading at a 14-week RSI value of 73, and the stock price is now significantly higher than the 50-week moving average in price. This is really a very obvious moment to trim your AMD stock.

The forward non-GAAP P/E and forward P/S ratios are now above the five-year average, with forward revenue growth a substantial decline from the five-year average (-26.5%), with forward diluted EPS growth a -21% difference from the five-year average. In short, AMD is fundamentally overvalued. Reduce your stake now as gains from here will be momentum based (value investors have already locked in profits and are looking elsewhere).

AMD is suitable for long-term holding but the likelihood of a drawdown in the near term is high before continued capital appreciation. 2026 will likely be strong for AMD, but I'm really anticipating an equity pullback in H2 if rates stay elevated and the Treasury General Account ("TGA") is about to be refilled with about $500 billion. A liquidity drain like this through H2 2025 has a dual purpose of refinancing the government's checking account and also stabilizing valuations before rate cuts in 2026 and a real economy, stable (not sentiment-driven) bull market (likely around the midterms).

In other words, you should be trimming your AI stocks now and raising cash (I'm currently at about 25-30%) and investing in undervalued names that are less prone to heavy volatility or defensive stocks like Eli Lilly (LLY), which has lower drawdown risk but strong 2026 potential, especially amid orforglipron (oral GLP-1) arriving to market next year. It's time to subtly reduce, rotate, and hold cash to strike amid more favorable tech valuations in H2—AMD included.

The reason that the above chart says "Trim" and not "Close" is that AMD's medium-term growth thesis is very much intact. This is not like mid-2024 when the valuation was extreme, it's moderately overextended. The pullback should be moderate, and then medium-term strong capital appreciation of 15% per annum is logical to expect. That said, that's not enough for top investors and I closed my position accordingly.

Operational Factors & Catalysts To Monitor At Q2 2025 Next Week

In a bull outcome, we might see the stock etch higher after Q2 earnings on August 5, but the valuation makes this somewhat improbable or just irrational from the market if it occurs. A strong beat and raise would be required, and some positive news on China like license approvals and renewed shipments would help. AI demand is soaring though, as major hyperscalers continue to double down on AI capex, so I don't expect any underperformance really. The bear case is a negligible risk (earnings miss and lowered guidance).

In my opinion, even if AMD delivers revenue and EPS in-line with expectations or even slightly above, the stock will take a moderate tumble. I think management will deliver a guidance raise, and it could be substantial, but consensus already reflects quite strong expectations, and the probability of raised guidance is already reflected in the stock price. That's clear when a 14-week RSI goes up above 70.

The weak PC market could also be a meaningful drag on overall performance that constrains further positive sentiment. Additional inventory write-downs from China would be a bearish quality, but this is not likely, as CEO Lisa Su has outlined that the China issue was "well-contained" and already guided for an $800 million inventory charge for MI308 GPUs in Q2. Realistically, we should expect no extra inventory charges above the $800 million previously outlined.

Conclusion: Hold

In my opinion, AMD stock is too expensive right now. It's definitely not a buy as the valuation has become overextended. I sold my entire position, but may buy it back if the valuation becomes more appealing in the next six months. I still think a 12-month price target of $200 is achievable, which was my old take-profit target before closing out ~$180. For long-term investors, trimming is fine, but for active traders and value players like myself, it's best to deploy the capital elsewhere right now because the implied 12-month upside is only 11% in a bull case.

There are other stocks I'm looking at right now which have better value prospects, like BYD (OTCPK:BYDDY), AppLovin (APP) (sentiment-based value, not intrinsic, even though forward fundamental growth is remarkable), and I am considering increasing my Nebius (NBIS) position as the stock has shown better sentiment-based value right now compared to the more dominant semi and AI stocks.

The macroeconomic climate for H2 2025 is still somewhat uncertain as I alluded to earlier in this analysis. My cash position is currently 29.5%, which is a bit high, but positions me well for any contraction that may occur in tech valuations soon.
